---
title: "Research Proposal — Grade-Level Drift in LLM-Generated Instruction for K-12 Academic Standards"
author: "Standards Research Group"
date: "2026-05-04"
date-format: "iso"
subtitle: "Proposal v0.1 — draft for internal review; pre-registration finalized before pilot run"
bibliography: references.bib
csl: csl/apa.csl
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    linestretch: 1.15
    colorlinks: true
---

# Abstract {.unnumbered}

State K-12 academic standards are increasingly used as the conditioning input to AI-mediated tutoring and content-generation systems. A standard like *3.NF.A.1* says "explain a fraction as a quantity formed by parts of a whole" — and the implicit promise of an AI tutor is that it will explain that idea *to a third grader*, in language a third grader can read. Whether large language models (LLMs) actually meet that promise is, surprisingly, an empirical question that the published literature has not directly answered.

This proposal lays out a study to measure **grade-level drift** in LLM-generated instructional text. For a stratified random sample of K-12 academic standards drawn from the Learning Commons (LC) Knowledge Graph (math and English Language Arts, K-12, multiple jurisdictions), we prompt three frontier OpenAI models — `gpt-5.5`, `gpt-5.4`, and `gpt-4.1` — to generate short student-facing explanations of each standard targeted at the standard's grade level, under a factorial design that crosses three prompt sizes (S/M/L) with two wording conditions (raw CCSS vs. simplified). We then score each output with a fully deterministic, open-source instrumentation stack: a seven-formula readability ensemble (Flesch-Kincaid, SMOG, Coleman-Liau, ARI, Gunning Fog, Dale-Chall, New Dale-Chall), a ridge-regression predictor calibrated against the CLEAR expert-annotated corpus, a vocabulary-tier profile (AWL / NGSL / off-list) and surface lexical statistics, and a spaCy-derived syntactic-complexity profile (mean dependency depth, T-unit length, clause and passive ratios). The primary outcome is the signed deviation between predicted and target grade. Adjacent literature on controllable readability [@imperial2023flesch; @ribeiro2023generating; @trott2024measuring] predicts compression toward the middle of the grade range, with mean absolute error in the 1-2 grade-level region; this proposal pre-registers that prediction.

The contribution is twofold: an empirical characterization of where, by how much, and for which standards LLMs miss the target reading level; and a reusable open methodology that grounds AI-tutoring quality claims in a measurable, instrument-based outcome rather than vibes. Findings are intended for a public blog publication and an open-source artifact release.

# Background and motivation

## The substrate question

State K-12 academic standards function as the *substrate* for AI-mediated learning. They are the structured input into curriculum-generation, item-generation, lesson-planning, and tutoring systems. Treating standards as substrate makes a strong implicit assumption: that an AI system, given a standard at grade N, will produce content that is *appropriate for grade N readers*. The standard's grade level is a hard constraint, not a soft suggestion — a fifth-grader who is handed sixth-grade prose does not silently up-level; they disengage.

The assumption that LLMs satisfy this constraint is widespread but largely unverified. AI-tutoring marketing claims grade-level appropriateness as a default; AI-content-generation pipelines add it as a prompt fragment ("write at a grade 5 reading level") and presume it works. The systematic empirical question — *how often, by how much, and on which kinds of standards do LLMs miss the target?* — has not been answered at scale in the K-12 instructional-text setting.

## Why now: the instrument exists

Reading-level measurement has historically been split between quantitative formulas (Flesch-Kincaid, Lexile, Dale-Chall, ATOS) — which capture word/sentence statistics but not meaning, abstraction, or knowledge demand — and expert qualitative judgment, which captures the missing dimensions but does not scale.

For the v0 question — "does generated text land at the target grade?" — the quantitative side of that split is in fact a fit-for-purpose instrument, provided two conditions are met. First, the formula stack must be wide enough that no single instrument's blind spot drives the headline; we use a seven-formula ensemble (Flesch-Kincaid, SMOG, Coleman-Liau, ARI, Gunning Fog, Dale-Chall, New Dale-Chall) and report the median as the ensemble grade. Second, the ensemble must be calibrated against expert-annotated K-12 instructional text rather than generic web prose; we train a ridge-regression predictor on the CLEAR corpus [@crossley2023largescaled] — the same corpus on which earlier LLM-as-judge evaluators were validated — and use it as a calibrated comparator alongside the raw ensemble. Qualitative dimensions (meaning/purpose, knowledge demand, register) are out of scope for v0 by design and deferred to Q5 (see §3.3); v0 trades the qualitative leg for full determinism, full reproducibility, and zero dependency on judge models.

## Why this is the right first study

This study is the first in a planned multi-part investigation of LLM behavior against K-12 standards. It is deliberately the first because:

- **Measurement is well-defined.** Drift between predicted and target grade is a single signed scalar with established analytic conventions.
- **No human-subjects data is required.** No IRB, no consent, no privacy considerations beyond standard secrets handling.
- **The result is publishable in either direction.** A finding of "no systematic drift" disconfirms a widespread informal assumption and is useful; a finding of structured drift is even more useful, with direct implications for prompt engineering, model selection, and content QA pipelines.
- **It establishes the reusable measurement scaffolding** that subsequent studies (entropy, interaction-data-grounded learning outcomes) will build on.

## Position in the broader research line

This study is the first in a planned multi-part investigation of LLM behavior against K-12 academic standards. Two follow-up investigations are sketched: one on per-standard model uncertainty and hallucination (using LLM internals and self-consistency to predict where instruction will go wrong), and one that loops in student interaction data to ground reading-level findings in actual learning outcomes. Each builds methodologically on the previous, so getting the measurement scaffolding right here is a load-bearing decision for the entire line.

# Research questions

## Central question

**For an LLM asked to generate student-facing instruction for a K-12 academic standard at grade N, does the produced text land at grade N's reading level — or does it systematically drift?**

## v0 sub-questions (this study answers these)

1. **Does drift exist?** Direction (above or below target grade) and magnitude.
2. **Is drift consistent across models?** Does each model carry its own bias?
3. **Are there standards that reliably produce above-grade output across all models — and what do those have in common?** (Standard wording reading-level? Topic? Vocabulary tier? Abstraction?)

## Sub-questions deferred to follow-ups

4. **Mechanism.** Is the model mirroring the standard's reading level, applying a topic-specific default register, or sensitive to prompt phrasing?
5. **Beyond reading level.** Sentence structure, vocabulary tier, conventionality, cohesion — orthogonal failure modes.
6. **Decoupling.** Do reading-level mismatch and subject-matter accuracy come apart?
7. **Loop closure with interaction data.** Does reading-level mismatch correlate with where students get stuck? (Deferred to a future investigation that pairs evaluator scores with student interaction data.)

This narrowing is deliberate. v0 establishes the descriptive finding and the measurement scaffolding; mechanism and outcome-grounded follow-ups are higher-investment and depend on having a baseline drift signal to anchor on.

# Hypotheses

The hypotheses below are pre-registered before the pilot run. Effect-size benchmarks follow the K-12 literature [@hill2008empirical] rather than Cohen's generic conventions, since standardized reading growth is ~0.32 SD per grade in middle grades — Cohen's "small" of 0.2 corresponds to two-thirds of a year of growth, far from negligible in this domain.

**H1 (drift exists, direction).** Mean signed deviation Δ = predicted_grade − target_grade is non-zero, where `predicted_grade` is the ensemble grade (median across the seven readability formulas; see §6.4). Based on @imperial2023flesch, @ribeiro2023generating, and @trott2024measuring, we predict **compression toward the middle**: positive Δ for low-grade standards (output is above-target) and negative Δ for high-grade standards (output is below-target).

**H2 (magnitude).** Mean absolute |Δ| is in the 1.0–2.0 grade-level range. Tighter than this would constitute strong evidence of grade calibration; wider than this would imply substantial regression-to-mean dynamics.

**H3 (within-family cross-model consistency).** Across the three OpenAI models in v0 (`gpt-5.5`, `gpt-5.4`, `gpt-4.1`), the *direction* of drift is preserved (same compression pattern), but *magnitude* differs by ≥ 0.5 grade levels between at least one model pair in at least one band. Equivalently: within-family model choice matters quantitatively but not qualitatively. Cross-*family* generalization (e.g., to Claude or Gemini) is explicitly out of v0 scope and is the central question of a planned follow-up.

**H4 (per-standard predictability — observational and interventional).** The model partially mirrors the input register. v0 tests this from two complementary angles within the same study:

- *Observational arm.* Standards whose own statement text is at the highest reading level (e.g., 9–12 standards using abstract verbs and Tier-3 vocabulary) will show the smallest |Δ|; standards at K-2 with a high prevalence of Tier-2 academic vocabulary inside the standard wording will show the largest upward drift. Operationalized as a regression of |Δ| on the standard's own ensemble grade.
- *Interventional arm.* Each standard is presented in two wording conditions: raw CCSS text vs. a simplified rewrite (produced once per standard by a separate LLM call to roughly fourth-grade reading level; the rewriter is cached so each standard's two arms are paired). H4 predicts that simplified wording reduces upward drift on K-2/3-5 standards, with the within-standard difference (raw − simplified) positive on average in those bands.

A consistent observational + interventional signature is much stronger evidence for the mirror-the-input-register mechanism than either arm alone.

**H5 (null option).** No systematic drift exists; observed |Δ| is statistically indistinguishable from the cross-formula spread of the deterministic ensemble (i.e., within the noise floor of the instrumentation itself). This is the disconfirmatory outcome and is reportable on its own merit.

# Literature review

The four themes below frame the empirical and methodological context for studying grade-level drift in LLM-generated instructional text. Where evidence is thin or contested, this is flagged explicitly rather than glossed.

## Theme A — Text-complexity and reading-level metrics

Quantitative readability formulas have a century of history but remain contested instruments. **Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level** [@kincaid1975derivation] and the related **Flesch Reading Ease** combine average sentence length and syllables per word into a grade estimate; the formula was developed for U.S. Navy training manuals and was never validated for narrative or instructional text aimed at children. It remains the default in word processors and many ed-tech pipelines despite well-documented failure modes on short, dialogic, or poetic text [@begeny2014can]. Relevance: F-K is the most likely "sanity-check" comparator a reviewer will request alongside our SAP-derived evaluators, so we report it even though we do not treat it as ground truth.

**Lexile Framework** [@stenner2006how] uses word frequency (against a large reference corpus) and sentence length, calibrated through Rasch modeling against a bank of cloze items. Lexile is the dominant metric in U.S. K-12 publishing and is reported on most state assessments. Its main limitation, acknowledged by MetaMetrics, is that it captures quantitative dimensions only — it cannot distinguish a complex idea expressed in short sentences from a simple idea expressed in long ones. Relevance: any cross-walk between our evaluator scores and "industry" reading levels will probably be evaluated by reviewers against Lexile.

**Dale-Chall** [@chall1995readability] uses a curated list of ~3,000 words familiar to fourth-graders plus average sentence length. **ATOS** [@renaissance2000atos] underlies the Accelerated Reader program and is calibrated on millions of student reading logs. Both are quantitative and share Lexile's blind spots. Relevance: ATOS is widely used in elementary classroom libraries, so for K-5 standards our outputs are de facto judged against ATOS-shaped intuitions.

The **Student Achievement Partners (SAP) Qualitative Text Complexity rubric** [@sap2013qualitative] emerged from the Common Core State Standards' Appendix A "three-legged stool" model of complexity (quantitative, qualitative, reader-and-task). SAP's rubric operationalizes the qualitative leg across four dimensions: meaning/purpose, structure, language conventionality and clarity, and knowledge demands. Relevance: SAP frames the dimensions of complexity that v0 *does not* measure (and is honest about — see T1). Q5 follow-up work that extends to qualitative scoring would adopt SAP as its construct backbone.

**Beck's vocabulary tiers** [@beck2013bringing] classify words as Tier 1 (basic, oral), Tier 2 (high-utility academic), or Tier 3 (domain-specific). Tier 2 vocabulary is the focus of most K-12 instructional planning. Relevance: our vocabulary-tier scoring block (AWL / NGSL / off-list) operationalizes this distinction directly — AWL is a close empirical proxy for Tier 2, and off-list serves as a Tier 3 proxy. Framing findings in tier language will be legible to educators.

**Coh-Metrix** [@graesser2004cohmetrix; @mcnamara2014automated] provides ~100 indices of cohesion, including referential overlap, latent semantic analysis-based connectedness, and connective density. Coh-Metrix demonstrated empirically that "easier" text is not always more cohesive — sometimes coherence demands richer connectives — undermining the assumption that simple = appropriate. Relevance: justifies why our evaluator suite is multi-dimensional (sentence structure separate from conventionality separate from knowledge demand) rather than a single scalar.

A useful synthesis is @nelson2012measures: they compared seven quantitative formulas against expert grade-band placements and found inter-formula agreement reasonable in middle grades but poor at K-2 and 11-CCR boundaries — exactly the boundaries where K-12 instructional decisions are highest-stakes. This empirically motivates qualitative supplementation.

## Theme B — LLM-as-judge validity (and why v0 does not use it)

The line of work most cited in our space starts with **Zheng et al.** [-@zheng2023judging], *Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena*. They documented that GPT-4 agreement with human pairwise preferences reached ~85%, comparable to human-human agreement, but identified four systematic biases: position bias (preference for the first-presented response), verbosity bias (preference for longer responses), self-enhancement bias (models prefer their own outputs), and limited reasoning on math/logic. Relevance: every one of these biases would be a plausible confound in an LLM-as-judge instrument scoring our outputs — verbosity bias in particular is structurally damaging here, since longer text correlates with higher classical grade level and a verbosity-biased judge would conflate "more text" with "higher grade." This concern, taken together with @panickssery2024llm on self-preference and the absence of peer-reviewed validation of LLM judges for K-12 grade-level scoring (see Henkel et al. below), is the reason v0 uses a fully deterministic instrumentation stack instead of any LLM judge.

**Verga et al.** [-@verga2024replacing], *Replacing Judges with Juries* (preprint), proposed PoLL (Panel of LLM Judges) ensembles using diverse smaller models, showing they reduce intra-model bias and cost relative to a single GPT-4 judge while maintaining or exceeding human-correlation. Relevance: even an ensemble of LLM judges does not fully escape the family-overlap problem when generators and judges are drawn from overlapping vendor pools; the deterministic v0 stack sidesteps the question entirely. PoLL remains the design template we would adopt if a future study adds qualitative evaluation.

**Kim et al.** [-@kim2024prometheus], *Prometheus 2*, trained dedicated 7B/8x7B evaluator models on rubric-grounded feedback and showed they approach GPT-4 evaluation performance on direct-assessment tasks. The line of work emphasizes that providing an explicit rubric is the single strongest factor in evaluator-human agreement. Relevance: anchors the design space for any qualitative follow-up to v0 (Q5); informs a future study, not v0 itself.

**Wang et al.** [-@wang2024large], *Large Language Models are not Fair Evaluators*, quantified position bias as flipping up to 50% of pairwise judgments under candidate reordering and proposed simple mitigations (multiple-evidence calibration, balanced position calibration). Relevance: position and scale-use biases are both irrelevant to a deterministic formula stack; this is one concrete benefit of the v0 instrument choice.

**Panickssery et al.** [-@panickssery2024llm], *LLM Evaluators Recognize and Favor Their Own Generations* (preprint), showed that frontier evaluators score outputs from their own model family higher than equivalent outputs from competitors, even controlling for quality. Relevance: this self-preference effect is the single strongest argument against using any LLM judge alongside v0's three OpenAI generators — an OpenAI-backed judge would systematically favor OpenAI generations, biasing within-family comparisons. The deterministic v0 instrumentation has no model identity and therefore no self-preference channel.

**Educationally-specific LLM-as-judge work is genuinely thin.** **Henkel et al.** [-@henkel2024can] used an LLM judge to evaluate Khan Academy tutor responses and reported moderate-to-good agreement with expert raters on dimensions like correctness and pedagogical appropriateness, but cautioned that agreement collapsed on more subjective dimensions. We are not aware of peer-reviewed work specifically validating LLM judges for grade-level / text-complexity assessment in K-12 contexts. The combination of "no validated K-12 LLM judge" plus the biases above is what pushes v0 to a deterministic stack; a validated judge for the qualitative dimensions (Q5) is a contribution opportunity for a follow-up study.

## Theme C — LLM register, complexity, and reading-level mismatch

Empirical work on whether LLMs hit a *requested* reading level is surprisingly limited. **Imperial and Madabushi** [-@imperial2023flesch], *Flesch or Fumble?*, prompted GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and several open models to generate text at specified Flesch-Kincaid grade levels and found systematic compression toward a middle band — outputs targeted for grade 2 came out closer to grade 5, and outputs targeted for grade 12 came out closer to grade 9. This is the closest direct precedent for our "drift" hypothesis. Relevance: predicts the direction (compression toward the middle) and provides a methodological template, though they evaluated free generation, not standards-anchored generation.

**Ribeiro et al.** [-@ribeiro2023generating], *Generating Summaries with Controllable Readability Levels*, showed that fine-tuning and instruction-tuning both improve readability control but neither achieves tight calibration; mean absolute error in F-K grade remained ~2 grades even for state-of-the-art systems. Relevance: bounds our prior expectation — drift of less than ~1 grade would actually be surprising given this baseline.

**Trott and Rivière** [-@trott2024measuring], *Measuring and Modifying the Readability of English Texts with GPT-4*, found GPT-4 could rewrite texts to be measurably easier or harder but with substantial variance and a strong regression-to-mean effect: extreme target levels were systematically missed. Relevance: same compression pattern, different domain, increases our confidence the effect is real.

For K-12 specifically, **Stowe et al.** [-@stowe2024controllable] and contemporaneous work on "controllable text simplification" (e.g., @kew2022targetlevel) consistently show that controllable simplification systems trained on Newsela and OneStopEnglish corpora over-simplify when targeting elementary grades and under-simplify when targeting upper-secondary — but most of this work pre-dates frontier instruction-tuned models. The specifically K-12 instructional-text question is, to our knowledge, **not directly addressed in peer-reviewed literature**. We flag this honestly: our study contributes to a thin evidentiary base.

**Padmakumar and He** [-@padmakumar2024does], *Does Writing with Language Models Reduce Content Diversity?*, is tangentially relevant — they showed LLM assistance compresses lexical and stylistic variance in human writing, consistent with a "pull toward the mean" mechanism that could explain compression toward a middle grade band.

## Theme D — LLMs as educational content generators and tutors

**Henkel et al.** [-@henkel2024can], *Can large language models make the grade?*, evaluated GPT-4 marking of K-12 short-answer responses against expert markers and found Cohen's κ in the 0.6-0.75 range — "substantial" but below typical inter-human agreement. Relevance: establishes a credibility benchmark for LLM-graded-against-human work in K-12 and provides an effect-size anchor.

**Khan Academy / Khanmigo evaluations** are largely industry-published. Khan Academy's 2024 impact reports describe pilot deployments but published peer-reviewed evidence is scarce. **Kestin et al.** [-@kestin2024ai], *AI Tutoring Outperforms Active Learning* (working paper, Harvard), reported a randomized comparison in a Harvard physics course finding LLM tutoring matched or exceeded active-learning instruction on short-term learning outcomes — a striking but single-context result. Relevance: shows the field is moving toward outcomes-grounded evaluation, but most published evidence remains either model-vs-model or self-reported.

**LearnLM** [@learnlm2024learnlm] is an instruction-tuned model line specifically post-trained for pedagogical behaviors; their public report includes human-rater comparisons against GPT-4 on pedagogy-specific rubrics with claimed wins on most dimensions. Relevance: signals that "pedagogical alignment" is treated as a distinct optimization target by frontier labs, supporting our premise that off-the-shelf LLMs are not automatically grade-calibrated.

**Macina et al.** [-@macina2023mathdial], *MathDial*, highlighted that LLM tutors frequently give away answers, fail to scaffold, and hallucinate intermediate reasoning steps. Relevance: pedagogical correctness and grade-level appropriateness are genuinely orthogonal failures, which is why v0 measures only the latter and explicitly defers subject-matter accuracy to Q6 follow-up work.

**Pardos and Bhandari** [-@pardos2024chatgpt], *ChatGPT-generated help produces learning gains equivalent to human tutor-authored help*, is one of the few peer-reviewed comparisons using actual student-outcome data (in an algebra learning system). Relevance: a useful counterpoint to purely model-vs-model evidence and a model for how outcomes-grounded follow-up work to ours might be designed.

Finally, **the standards-alignment literature is sparse**. Most published work on LLM-generated instructional content focuses on Q&A correctness or hallucination rates rather than alignment to specific state K-12 standards. @sonkar2023class evaluated LLM-generated math hints for correctness but did not score grade-level fit. We flag this gap as a contribution opportunity.

## Methodology references

Our design choices draw on established practice in educational measurement and the emerging LLM-evaluation literature.

**Stratified random sampling of educational items.** Stratification by grade and subject is standard in educational measurement; the AERA/APA/NCME *Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing* [@aera2014standards] treat representative coverage of the construct domain (here: grade × subject × strand) as a basic validity requirement. Item-sampling designs in studies like NAEP framework documents [@nces2022naep] use multi-stage stratified sampling across content strands and cognitive complexity levels — we follow that template. For LLM evaluation specifically, **Liang et al.** [-@liang2023holistic], *Holistic Evaluation of Language Models*, argued explicitly for stratified scenario sampling to avoid skewed conclusions when a model's failure modes are concentrated in a subdomain.

**LLM evaluation experimental design.** **Zheng et al.** [-@zheng2023judging] established the practice of running each evaluation under multiple seeds and reporting variance. **Biderman et al.** [-@biderman2024lessons], *Lessons from the Trenches* (preprint), provided a thorough catalog of reproducibility hazards: prompt sensitivity, decoding-parameter sensitivity, evaluator-version drift, and tokenization edge cases. Our paired design — same standard, multiple prompts, multiple seeds, multiple evaluator runs — mirrors their recommendations.

**Statistics for detecting drift.** Our primary outcome is a signed deviation (evaluator score minus target grade), supporting both directional (compression) and magnitude tests. Effect-size conventions follow **Cohen** [-@cohen1988statistical] for *d* and **Hedges and Olkin** [-@hedges1985statistical] for the small-sample-corrected *g*. In education research specifically, **Hill et al.** [-@hill2008empirical] in *Child Development Perspectives* recommended interpreting effect sizes against empirical benchmarks (the average grade-to-grade growth on standardized reading assessments is ~0.32 SD in middle grades), which is the reference frame we use rather than Cohen's generic "small/medium/large" labels — these substantially under-call effects in education contexts.

For agreement between instruments and across the within-formula spread of the readability ensemble, **Cohen's κ** [@cohen1960coefficient] for categorical agreement and **intraclass correlation (ICC)** following **Shrout and Fleiss** [-@shrout1979intraclass] and **Koo and Li** [-@koo2016guideline] for continuous scores are standard. We will report ICC(2,k) across the seven readability formulas (treating each formula as a "rater" applied to a random sample of outputs) as a measure of within-ensemble agreement, and Pearson r between the ensemble and the CLEAR-calibrated ridge as a between-instrument convergent-validity metric. The deterministic stack itself is byte-reproducible across runs, so there is no test-retest ICC to report for the instrument. **McHugh** [-@mchugh2012interrater] provides commonly-cited interpretive bands for κ in clinical and educational contexts.

**SAP/CLEAR provenance.** The SAP Qualitative Text Complexity rubric [@sap2013qualitative] was developed to operationalize Common Core Appendix A's qualitative dimension and has been used in dozens of state-level curriculum review processes; v0 does not use it directly but cites it as the construct frame that defines the boundary of what a quantitative-only stack can and cannot measure (T1). The **CLEAR corpus** (CommonLit Ease of Readability) was released by **Crossley et al.** [-@crossley2023largescaled] in *Behavior Research Methods*: ~5,000 excerpts from grades 3-12 paired with expert pairwise complexity judgments, designed specifically to support training and validation of computational readability models. v0 uses CLEAR directly as the training corpus for the calibrated ridge predictor that complements the seven-formula ensemble; we cite the CLEAR paper as primary justification for the ridge predictor's external validity.

# Methodology

The full operational specification is in [`methodology.md`](methodology.md); this section summarizes the design and adds the pre-registration commitments and statistical-power considerations.

## Design overview

A **fully crossed within-standard factorial** design. Each sampled standard at target grade N is fed through the pipeline once per (model × prompt-size × wording) cell — 3 × 3 × 2 = 18 cells per standard. Within each cell, generation runs once at temperature 0 (deterministic decoding), and each output is scored by the full deterministic instrumentation stack (§6.4). The unit of analysis is (standard × model × prompt-size × wording); drift is operationalized as the signed difference Δ = predicted_grade − target_grade, where `predicted_grade` is the seven-formula ensemble grade. The CLEAR-calibrated ridge predictor, the vocabulary-tier profile, and the syntactic-complexity profile contribute decompositional and convergent-validity signal.

## Data and sampling

- **Source.** LC Knowledge Graph (REST API for the pilot; public JSONL snapshot for the published artifact). LC is used here only as the standards catalogue; v0 has no other dependence on LC infrastructure.
- **Frame.** Records with `normalizedStatementType == "Standard"` — the leaf-learning-expectation set in CASE-format frameworks. Includes `statementType` values `Standard`, `Component`, and `Content Standard`; excludes `Standard Grouping` rows (Strands, Domains, Clusters, Conceptual Categories, Grade-Level headers).
- **Parent sample (n = 100 per subject; 200 total; drawn 2026-05-04).** Simple random per subject, seed `20260504`, drawn from Multi-State CCSS only (ELA + Math). Eligible population at draw: 1100 ELA + 597 Math. Artifact: [`data/processed/pilot_v1_sample.json`](../data/processed/pilot_v1_sample.json). The parent sample is the pool from which v0 and any later expansion draw.
- **v0 sub-sample (n = 30 per subject; 60 total).** Drawn without replacement from the parent sample with seed `20260504`, stratified by grade band (K-2 / 3-5 / 6-8 / 9-12) where parent-sample composition allows. With 60 standards × 18 cells = **1,080 generations** total, v0 has > 0.85 power to detect a within-band Δ of 0.5 grade levels (paired-by-standard tests, α = 0.05) — the smallest band-level effect this study is designed to call.
- **Full study sample (n ≈ 300; planned for a follow-up phase).** Strata: (Grade band: K-2, 3-5, 6-8, 9-12) × (Subject: ELA, Math) × (Jurisdiction: 3-5 anchor states + Multi-State / CCSS). Equal allocation per cell. Triggered only if v0 surfaces an effect that warrants jurisdiction- and stratum-resolved estimation.
- **Random seed.** Pinned and recorded per run; the sampled identifier list is committed alongside the run manifest.

## Generation conditions

The 18-cell factorial design crosses three orthogonal factors. Each generation runs at temperature 0 with the model ID and prompt SHA pinned in the run manifest.

| Factor | Levels | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Model | `gpt-5.5`, `gpt-5.4`, `gpt-4.1` | Three OpenAI models. Within-family by design; cross-family (Claude, Gemini) is the deferred follow-up that tests H3 generalization. |
| Prompt size | S (~50 tokens), M (~150 tokens), L (~400 tokens) | S = standard text + grade target + length constraint. M = + role context (teacher) + format instructions, no examples. L = + one worked exemplar (one-shot). |
| Wording | Raw CCSS, Simplified | Simplified wording is produced once per standard by a separate LLM rewrite to roughly fourth-grade reading level; the rewriter is cached so the two arms for a given standard are paired (same source, two register conditions). |

Per-standard cell count: 3 × 3 × 2 = 18. Total v0 generations: 60 × 18 = 1,080. The simplified-wording arm directly serves H4's interventional test (§3); the prompt-size and model arms together serve H1, H2, and H3.

## Scoring

Each generated output is scored by a fully deterministic, open-source instrumentation stack. There is no LLM-as-judge anywhere in the v0 pipeline. The four scoring blocks are:

| Block | Output features | Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| Reading level — ensemble (headline) | Flesch-Kincaid, SMOG, Coleman-Liau, ARI, Gunning Fog, Dale-Chall, New Dale-Chall; **median across formulas** as the ensemble grade | `textstat` |
| Reading level — calibrated | Single grade prediction from a ridge regression trained on the CLEAR corpus, features = the ensemble formula stack above | `scikit-learn` ridge fit; CLEAR splits and seed pinned in the run manifest |
| Vocabulary tier | % AWL (Coxhead Academic Word List), % NGSL (New General Service List), % off-list (Tier-3 proxy), mean word length, type-token ratio | Wordlist matching against the published AWL/NGSL/Dale-Chall lists |
| Syntactic complexity | Mean dependency depth, T-unit length (L2SCA-style), subordinate-clause ratio, passive ratio, nominalization ratio | spaCy dependency parse |
| Surface | Word / sentence / paragraph counts, input + output token counts | Run-time logging |

Because every block is deterministic given fixed inputs and pinned package versions, re-running scoring on the same generated text produces byte-identical output; there is no test-retest stability question for the instrument itself. Test-retest variation in v0 is purely the within-standard variation across temperature-0 model generations across cells, which is the substantive signal we want to characterize.

Scores are stored as `(run_id, standard_id, model, prompt_size, wording, feature_name, value, instrument_version)`. No score is overwritten; rerunning produces a new `run_id`.

## Statistical analysis

**Primary analyses (pre-registered).**

1. Mean signed Δ within each grade band, aggregated across the 18 cells per standard with 95% confidence intervals. Single-sample *t*-test against zero per band; Holm-Bonferroni corrected across bands.
2. Direction-of-drift test: sign test on Δ aggregated within (low: K-5) and (high: 6-12) groups, expecting positive Δ in low and negative Δ in high under H1.
3. Mean |Δ| overall and per band, with 95% CI; tested against the H2 prediction of 1.0–2.0 grade levels.
4. Within-family cross-model contrast (H3): Δ regressed on model with standard as a random intercept, expecting a non-zero model main effect (≥ 0.5 grade levels between at least one model pair in at least one band) but no qualitative reversal of the compression direction.
5. H4 interventional contrast: paired within-standard difference Δ_raw − Δ_simplified, tested per band with a paired *t*-test, expecting a positive difference in K-2 / 3-5 (simplified wording reduces upward drift).

**Secondary analyses.**

6. H4 observational arm: regress |Δ| on the standard's own ensemble grade, controlling for model, prompt size, and wording. Sign and magnitude of the slope test the mirror-the-input-register prediction.
7. Per-standard drift profile: standards with mean |Δ| > 1.0 across all 18 cells are identified; their statement-text features (length, ensemble grade, vocabulary tier composition) are regressed on |Δ| as a Q3 / H4 follow-up.
8. Decomposition: vocabulary-tier and syntactic-complexity features regressed on Δ, to attribute drift to lexical vs syntactic contributions.
9. Convergent-validity cross-check: ensemble grade vs CLEAR-calibrated ridge prediction, reported per band as a Pearson r and a Bland-Altman plot.

**Exploratory cuts.** Anything else (subject × Δ interactions beyond ELA-vs-Math main effect, prompt-size × wording interactions, per-formula disagreement patterns, etc.) is reported as exploratory and not used for headline claims.

## Pre-registration commitments

Before the v0 run begins, the following are frozen and recorded in a tagged commit (`pre-reg-v1`):

- The parent sample frame and seed; the v0 sub-sample identifier list.
- All three prompt templates (S/M/L), each SHA recorded.
- The wording-rewriter prompt (SHA recorded) and the cached simplified-wording artifact for each sampled standard.
- The three OpenAI model IDs and decoding parameters (temperature 0).
- The instrumentation-stack package versions: `textstat`, `spacy` (and the model name + version), `scikit-learn`, the AWL / NGSL / Dale-Chall list versions, and the CLEAR-calibrated ridge predictor's training split, seed, and serialized weights.
- The five primary analyses listed above with their specific test statistics and α levels.
- The H1–H4 predictions, in advance of seeing results.

The parent sample (n=100 per subject; 200 total; CC-only) was drawn 2026-05-04 to support pipeline development and is allowed to influence the freeze (e.g., adjusting output length, refining prompts, deciding HS multi-grade handling, ratifying the population frame). The v0 run, on the n=60 sub-sample, executes after the freeze.

# Reproducibility

This study is designed so a reader with no privileged access can replicate the entire pipeline. The shift to a deterministic instrumentation stack means everything past the generation step is byte-reproducible from the released artifacts alone.

- **Standards data.** Public JSONL snapshot pinned at the time of publication. The snapshot date and file hashes are recorded in `data/processed/run_manifest.json`. The LC REST API is used during development for speed but is not on the published reproduction path; LC is the standards catalogue, nothing else.
- **Code.** Open-source MIT-licensed (on public release). Includes the LC client, sampling, the three prompt templates, the wording-rewriter, the generation driver, the deterministic scoring stack, the CLEAR-calibrated ridge fit script, and the analysis notebook.
- **Generated artifacts.** All generated instruction texts (1,080 cells), all per-output feature scores, the cached simplified-wording rewrites, the serialized CLEAR-calibrated ridge model, the complete run manifest, and the analysis notebook are released alongside the publication. Storage: a release artifact attached to the corresponding GitHub release tag.
- **External dependencies the reader needs.** A single API key — `OPENAI_API_KEY` — for re-running the generation step. The CLEAR corpus (publicly available from CommonLit / Crossley et al.) is required to refit the ridge predictor; the serialized predictor is also released so refitting is optional. Python dependencies are limited to `textstat`, `spacy` (with the pinned `en_core_web_sm` model), `scikit-learn`, `pyarrow`, and `plotly`. No Gemini key, no Anthropic key, no LC API key for evaluation. Costs are estimable from the run manifest and are reported in the paper.
- **Determinism.** Scoring is fully deterministic given pinned package versions; re-running the scoring step on the released generations reproduces every reported number byte-identically. Generation at temperature 0 plus pinned model IDs is reproducible up to provider drift, which is logged via timestamped manifest entries.

The dual-license posture (MIT for code, CC BY 4.0 for data and prose; see [`LICENSE.md`](../LICENSE.md)) ensures all pieces can be vendored, cited, and remixed. Attribution requirements for upstream artifacts (LC, SAP, CLEAR, AWL/NGSL, 1EdTech, state DOEs) are listed in [`attribution.md`](attribution.md) and reproduced in any publication.

# Threats to validity

The threats below are surfaced from the literature review and from our own pre-mortem. Each is paired with a planned mitigation; some cannot be fully mitigated and remain as honest limitations.

**T1 — Deterministic-formula construct gap.** Classical readability formulas (and the seven-formula ensemble that anchors v0) capture surface features — word length, sentence length, word-list membership — but not register, abstraction, knowledge demand, or text structure. SAP-style qualitative dimensions are the part of complexity that best discriminates K-12 instructional fit, and v0 does not measure them. *Mitigation.* This is a deliberate scope decision, not an oversight: v0 measures reading level only and is framed that way throughout the paper. Qualitative dimensions are explicitly deferred to Q5 follow-up work, where a validated rubric-grounded judge (or rater study) is the appropriate instrument. Headline claims are scoped to "reading-level fit" and never to "instructional appropriateness" tout court.

**T2 — Ensemble blind spot if all formulas share a common bias.** The seven-formula ensemble protects against any single formula's idiosyncrasies, but every member is a surface-feature instrument; if all seven share a common blind spot (for example, all under-weight Tier-3 vocabulary outside the Dale-Chall list), the ensemble inherits that bias. *Mitigation.* Convergent validity is built into the v0 design as a complementary instrument: the CLEAR-calibrated ridge predictor is trained on expert pairwise complexity judgments and provides an external anchor. Each individual formula's prediction is reported alongside the ensemble per output, so readers can audit formula-level disagreement; the per-band Pearson r and Bland-Altman plot of ensemble-vs-ridge are part of the secondary analyses (§6.5).

**T3 — Output-type artifact.** Findings only generalize to the pinned 100–250-word explanation format. *Mitigation.* Acknowledged in scope.md and disclosed in the paper. A future study extends to lesson plans and worked examples.

**T4 — Standards-text confound.** A standard whose own wording is at grade 11 may push the model up regardless of the target grade. *Mitigation.* H4's two-arm design (observational + interventional) tests this directly. The interventional arm — within-standard raw vs simplified wording — is the strongest available test short of a controlled human-rewrite study. If the standard's own ensemble grade explains > 50% of |Δ| variance *and* the simplified-wording arm reduces drift by a comparable margin, the headline claim shifts from "LLMs drift" to "LLMs mirror the standard's register"; both are publishable, the framing changes.

**T5 — Rewriter-induced register confound.** The simplified-wording arm of H4 is produced by a separate LLM call, which has its own register and its own (smaller, but non-zero) version of the drift the study is trying to characterize. The rewrites could systematically inject a register that biases the simplified arm toward the rewriter's middle band rather than toward the target. *Mitigation.* The contrast of interest is *within-standard* (raw vs simplified for the same standard, same target grade), so any rewriter register that affects both arms similarly cancels out. We additionally characterize the rewriter outputs themselves with the same deterministic stack (ensemble grade, vocabulary tier, syntactic complexity) and report the distribution; if the rewrites cluster tightly at a single grade band well below the target, the paper reports the contrast as "register-shifted rewrite vs raw" rather than "fourth-grade rewrite vs raw" and adjusts the H4 interpretation accordingly.

**T6 — CLEAR is the only external calibration anchor.** The CLEAR-calibrated ridge is the convergent-validity comparator for the formula ensemble; CLEAR itself is one expert-annotated corpus and is not free of its own biases (genre coverage, age of source texts, rater pool composition). *Mitigation.* We report ensemble-vs-ridge agreement as a Bland-Altman plot per band so the reader can see where the two instruments disagree; we do not treat the CLEAR-calibrated ridge as ground truth. Where ensemble and ridge disagree by more than 1.0 grade level on a given output, that output is flagged in the released artifact for manual inspection in any follow-up.

**T7 — Collinearity within the deterministic stack.** The seven readability formulas, the vocabulary-tier features, and the syntactic-complexity features are not independent of one another (sentence length appears in multiple formulas; T-unit length and Flesch-Kincaid are functionally related). The decomposition analysis (§6.5) treats them as if they each carry distinct signal. *Mitigation.* A correlation matrix and a factor analysis on the full feature matrix are reported as a methodological subsidiary analysis, so the reader can see which features are redundant and weight the decomposition accordingly.

**T8 — Human-baseline ceiling.** Inter-rater agreement among expert teachers on grade-band placement is ~ICC 0.6-0.75 [@crossley2023largescaled; @nelson2012measures]. The v0 instrument is deterministic and so does not have a test-retest variance of its own, but its absolute calibration cannot exceed expert inter-rater agreement and remain meaningfully interpretable at sub-grade resolution. *Mitigation.* We report drift estimates with band-level resolution and do not over-claim sub-grade precision; the human-baseline ICC is reported alongside the v0 results for context.

**T9 — Prompt-sensitivity dominance vs model effects.** @biderman2024lessons and @wang2024large repeatedly show that prompt variation can exceed model variation. *Mitigation.* Prompt sensitivity is built into the v0 design as the prompt-size factor (S/M/L). The mixed-effects model in §6.5 tests whether the prompt-size main effect exceeds the within-family model main effect; if it does, the H3 finding is reported as "within-family-model-and-prompt-dependent" rather than "within-family-model-dependent."

**T10 — Provider drift.** Frontier model APIs evolve; the same model ID may produce subtly different outputs months apart. *Mitigation.* All generation timestamps are logged. Reruns of the published study, if attempted later, are expected to show provider-drift effects; we report this as a limitation of any LLM-based artifact. The deterministic scoring step is unaffected.

# Ethics and responsible disclosure

- **Human subjects.** The v0 study uses no student data, no human-rated content, and no PII. Standards text is public CC BY 4.0 material; LLM outputs are generated by the research team. No IRB review is required for v0.
- **Responsible AI disclosure.** Published artifacts disclose the full LLM stack used at generation time (the three OpenAI models with pinned IDs and API access dates), the wording-rewriter model and prompt, and the deterministic scoring stack with package versions. The paper includes an explicit statement that scoring contains no LLM-as-judge component and an honest accounting of what classical readability formulas do and do not capture (T1).
- **Misuse considerations.** Findings are cite-able in either direction: a "drift exists" finding could be used to argue that AI tutors are unsafe for K-12; a "no drift" finding could be used to argue the opposite. We mitigate over-claiming by publishing both the raw artifacts (per-standard scores, generated text) and the prose, so any claim can be cross-checked against the data.
- **Attribution and IP.** All upstream artifacts (LC for standards data; CLEAR / CommonLit for the calibration corpus; AWL, NGSL, and Dale-Chall list maintainers; spaCy and `textstat` maintainers; 1EdTech; state DOEs) are credited per [`attribution.md`](attribution.md). No proprietary content is included in the released artifacts.

# Deliverables and timeline

| Phase | Deliverable | Target date |
|---|---|---|
| Pipeline setup | `lc_client` working end-to-end; parent sample (n=100 per subject; 200 total; CC-only) drawn 2026-05-04; v0 sub-sample (n=30 per subject; 60 total) drawn from parent; deterministic scoring stack and CLEAR-calibrated ridge in place; wording-rewriter cached for all 60 standards | T+1 week |
| Pre-registration freeze | Sub-sample, three prompt SHAs, rewriter SHA, three model IDs, instrumentation-stack versions, primary analyses, H1–H4 — all committed and tagged `pre-reg-v1` | T+2 weeks |
| v0 run | Generation and scoring complete: 60 standards × 3 models × 3 prompts × 2 wordings = 1,080 cells | T+3 weeks |
| Analysis | Notebooks for primary, secondary, and exploratory analyses; per-standard drift profiles; ensemble-vs-CLEAR-ridge convergent-validity report | T+4 weeks |
| Internal review | Methodology + results reviewed against this proposal; threats-to-validity check; pre-publication adjustments only on exploratory analyses (primary remains pre-registered) | T+5 weeks |
| Blog publication | Public post; repo opened to MIT/CC-BY 4.0 license; release artifact attached to a tagged GitHub release | T+6 weeks |
| Follow-up | Cross-family generalization (Claude + Gemini), and a separate Q5 qualitative-rubric study, opened in sibling repos | T+8 weeks |

T = v0 kickoff date.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
